title: What’s a Risograph?
text:
This is a Risograph!



On the face of it, this is a fairly boring looking printer! What makes it interesting is the way it works. Which we can see if we open it up and look inside.



Inside, there are these two drums, which are used for printing.
Here we can see there are currently two colors in the printer yellow and magenta.1
1 Risographs can have between 1-4 colors, how many depends on how expensive the printer is! This is our first clue as to what is different!Looking at the printer drums themselves



The drum here is the entire print surface - which forms the size of the paper we are limited too when using the risograph.
In effect what a risograph is doing is semi-automating a process that is very similar to screen printing. Wherein stencil system is used to transfer designs onto a object of your choosing.



Screen printing was perhaps most famously used by Andy Warhol as part of his signature style.2
2 We also have this original Warhol screenprint currently hanging in the CIM Pink Room!


The Tate have a nice video showing how this works through replicating Warhol’s process.


However, as you can see this is quite a laborious process - with each additional color we use is an equal increase in the time it takes to create the final print.
This is why, when screenprinting is used for other products, such as t-shirts, they tend to make use of only one or two colors.
The risograph automates this process. Just like screen printing, a stencil is burnt inside the printer, and then our paper is run through.

title: One Color
text:
One of the things that differentiates riso graph printing is that it can give us constraints on what it is we can make.
Here are some examples of cool riso prints made using just one color:


















As our workshop with Stefanie Posavec having constraints on what we are able to use can unlock new ways of thinking about a problem or creative ways of expressing what it is we are trying to do!
Building off Stef’s work the challenge I am setting for todays class is to create our own Data Design Postcards.1
1 We are also creating postcards for a very practical reason, riso printing (because we need to create stencil screens and change ink drums) is only an economically viable prospect at Warwick (this will vary for other print studios) for minimum print runs of 35. By using postcards this means we can print everyones designs in a few runs an then share the results!These postcards can be anything you want so long as they encapsulate, demonstrate or publicise something you have learnt on this course.
So lets get started by creating our postcards and starting sketching!
First, fold up an A3 piece of paper into 8 sections.



Then I want you to take one color blue, yellow, pink/red, black and try to sketch out 4 designs in one of the four sections.2


2 Incidentally 8 sections folds are a way to make zine style ‘book’ which works very well for riso printing, see this video for how to do this: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AbFmnaU41KI

title: Data Design Camp 25/26: Creative Printing ft Risograph Machine
text:
Welcome to our second Data Design Camp!
Today over the next two (and a bit) hours we will be exploring printing our work. In particular, we will be looking at how we can print using a Risograph machine - which is not only a cool way to print, but opens up some of the complexities we can face when translating our work from digital to analogue.
Some quick notes

This website, and today’s class has a lot of content! I’ve broken this down into some exploratory exercises which we will be working through, however, there is far to much on this little website to be covered in this time. Please return to these materials as and when they are useful to you!
Risographs are quite an unusual way of printing so you might not ever encounter them again after this class - so don’t feel the need to go through all the materials!
After today’s class there will also be the opportunity to have some of your own work printed using the risograph. Again this is entirely voluntary, but is a fun opportunity to materialise some of your work - or anything else you fancy!
(and if you really hate the magenta pink there is a little icon under the title on the left hand side you can use to turn it off.)
crumbs: Welcome to our second Data Design Camp!
